Algorithm eﬃciency empirical analysis
(cid:73) Employed when the theoretical analysis is diﬃcult.
(cid:73) Aim:
(cid:73) formulation of an hypothesis regarding the algorithm eﬃciency;
(cid:73) veriﬁcation of this hypothesis;
(cid:73) comparison of algorithms;
(cid:73) eﬃciency analysis of some implementation.

Lecture 3

Algorithm eﬃciency empirical analysis
(cid:73) The analysis aim is set.
(cid:73) An eﬃciency measure is chosen
Example: elementary operation counting, running time, etc.
(cid:73) Input data characteristics are set.
(cid:73) The algorithm is implemented.
(cid:73) Input data are generated.
(cid:73) The program is run for for all input data; the result are stored.
(cid:73) The results are analyzed.

Recursive functions
(cid:73) A function f() calls directly a function g() if the deﬁnition of f()
contains at least one call to g().
(cid:73) A function f() calls indirectly a function g() if f() calls directly a
function h(), and h() calls directly or indirectly the function g().
(cid:73) A function f() is recursively deﬁned if it calls itself directly or
indirectly.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 3
DS 2018/2019
8 / 40

Recursive functions
A recursive function deﬁnition:
(cid:73) Base case testing – recursive calls stopping condition.
(cid:73) Recursive (general case): some variable (integer) is passed as
parameter to the function itself such that after a ﬁnite number of
calls to reach the base case.
Remark: There exists recursive function with no parameters.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 3
DS 2018/2019
9 / 40

Recursive functions
Example 1. Factorial function deﬁnition:
(cid:73) Base case: 0! = 1;
(cid:73) General case: n! = n × ((n − 1)!), n > 0.
Function factorial(n)
begin
if n <= 1 then
return 1
else
return (n ∗ factorial(n − 1))
end
FII, UAIC
Lecture 3
DS 2018/2019
10 / 40

(cid:73) recursive algorithms: easy to implement;
(cid:73) additional cost: each recursive call places information in a speciﬁc
memory zone (program stack).
factorial(4) - recursive calls
FII, UAIC
Lecture 3
DS 2018/2019
11 / 40

factorial(4) - recursive calls
(cid:73) recursive algorithms: easy to implement;
(cid:73) additional cost: each recursive call places information in a speciﬁc
memory zone (program stack).
FII, UAIC
Lecture 3

11 / 40

factorial(n) - iterative version
Function factorial(n)
begin
product ← 1
while n > 1 do
product ← product ∗ n
n ← n − 1
return product
end
FII, UAIC
Lecture 3
DS 2018/2019
12 / 40

Recursion vs. iteration. Recursive Fibonacci
Example 2. Fibonacci numbers:
(cid:73) f (0) = 0, f (1) = 1,
(cid:73) f (n) = f (n − 1) + f (n − 2), n > 1.
Function ﬁb(n)
begin
if n <= 1 then
return n
else
return ﬁb(n − 1) + ﬁb(n − 2)
end
FII, UAIC
Lecture 3
DS 2018/2019
13 / 40

Recursive Fibonacci: call tree
FII, UAIC
Lecture 3
DS 2018/2019
14 / 40
O(φn)

Recursion vs. iteration: iterative Fibonacci
Function iﬁb(n)
begin
f 0 ← 0
f 1 ← 1
if n <= 1 then
return n
else
for k ← 2 to n do
temp ← f 1
f 1 ← f 1 + f 0
f 0 ← temp
return f 1
end
FII, UAIC
Lecture 3
DS 2018/2019
16 / 40

Recursive algorithm eﬃciency
(cid:73) To estimate the running time:
(cid:73) a recurrence is set in order to express the relation between the
execution time of the initial problem and the execution time of the
reduced problem;
(cid:73) the recurrence is solved.
(cid:73) Example: for the factorial, the recurrence that estimates the running
time is:
T (n) =
(cid:26) 0,
n = 0
T (n − 1) + 1, n > 1
FII, UAIC
Lecture 3
DS 2018/2019
18 / 40

How to solve recurrences
1. Substitution method. A limit is guessed and is proved by induction.
2. Iteration method. The recurrence is iterated and expressed as a sum
of terms that depends only on the problem size and the initial
condition (base cases).
3. Recurrence tree. Expresses the recurrence as a tree (nodes
corresponds to costs).
4. Master theorem. Provides limits for recurrences of type
T (n) = aT (n/b) + f (n)
.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 3
DS 2018/2019
19 / 40

1. Substitution method
(cid:73) The solution is guessed.
(cid:73) Mathematical induction is employed to determine the constants and
to proof that the solution is correct.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 3
DS 2018/2019
21 / 40

(cid:73) Guessed solution: T (n) = O(n log n).
(cid:73) To be proved by induction: T (n) ≤ cn log n, for c > 0.
Suppose that the relation is true for all positive values m < n, in
particular for m = (cid:98)n/2(cid:99): T ((cid:98)n/2(cid:99)) ≤ c(cid:98)n/2(cid:99) log((cid:98)n/2(cid:99)).
T (n) ≤ 2(c(cid:98)n/2(cid:99) log((cid:98)n/2(cid:99))) + n
≤ cn log((cid:98)n/2(cid:99)) + n
= cn log n − cn log 2 + n
= cn log n − cn + n
≤ cn log n, for c ≥ 1
It has to be shown that the relation holds for the base case (limit
conditions). T (1) = 1 ≤ c1 log 1 = 0
Base cases: T(2) and T(3) ( n0 = 2 )
T (2) = 4 and T (3) = 5, T (2) ≤ c2 log 2 and
T (3) ≤ c3 log 3 ⇒ c ≥ 2.
Substitution method – example
Find an upper bound for the recurrence T (n) = 2T ((cid:98)n/2(cid:99)) + n
FII, UAIC
Lecture 3
DS 2018/2019

Substitution method – tips
(cid:73) Subtraction of an inferior term order (to consolidate the inductive
hypothesis).
Example: T (n) = T ((cid:98)n/2(cid:99)) + T ((cid:100)n/2(cid:101)) + 1
(cid:73) Guessed solution: T (n) = O(n).
(cid:73) It is shown by induction that T (n) ≤ cn, for c > 0.
T (n) ≤ c(cid:98)n/2(cid:99) + c(cid:100)n/2(cid:101) + 1
= cn + 1
(cid:73) It is shown by induction that T (n) ≤ cn − d, d >= 0 const.
T (n) ≤ (c(cid:98)n/2(cid:99) − d) + (c(cid:100)n/2(cid:101) − d) + 1
= cn − 2d + 1
≤ cn − d, for d ≥ 1
The c constant has to be chosen such that the limit conditions are
satisﬁed.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 3
DS 2018/2019
23 / 40

Substitution method – tips
(cid:73) Traps
Example: T (n) = 2T ((cid:98)n/2(cid:99)) + n
It is shown (“false”) that T (n) = O(n) guessing that T (n) ≤ cn and:
T (n) ≤ 2(c(cid:98)n/2(cid:99)) + n
≤ cn + n
= O(n), ⇐ false!!
Error: it was not proven the exact form of the induction hypothesis.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 3
DS 2018/2019
24 / 40

Substitution method – tips
(cid:73) Variable changes.
Example: T (n) = 2T ((cid:98)sqrt(n)(cid:99)) + log n
The recurrence is simpliﬁed by variable change: m = log n.
T (2m) = 2T (2m/2) + m
Rename S(m) = T (2m), and it follows S(m) = 2S(m/2) + m.
S(m) = O(m log m),
T (n) = T (2m) = S(m) = O(m log m) = O(log n log log n).
FII, UAIC
Lecture 3
DS 2018/2019
25 / 40

2. Recurrence iteration
Substitution method: implies to guess the solution (!)
Recurrence iteration:
(cid:73) direct
(cid:73) starts from the base case and builds successive terms using the
recurrence;
(cid:73) the expression of the general term T (n) is identiﬁed;
(cid:73) it is proved by direct computations or mathematical induction.
(cid:73) reverse
(cid:73) it starts from the T (n) and is replaced by T (h(n)) with corresponding
value, then T (h(h(n))) is replaced and so on until the base case is
reached;
(cid:73) T (n) is obtained.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 3
DS 2018/2019
27 / 40

Direct iteration
Reverse iteration
T (1) = 0
T (2) = 1
T (3) = 2
. . .
T (n) = n − 1
T (n) = T (n − 1) + 1
T (n − 1) = T (n − 2) + 1
. . .
T (2) = T (1) + 1
T (1) = 0
——————
T (n) = n − 1

T (n − 1) + 1, n > 1

3. Recurrence trees
(cid:73) Recurrence trees:
(cid:73) allows to visualize a recurrence;
(cid:73) each node corresponds to the cost of a sub-problem;
(cid:73) the costs are sum on levels and then they are summed to get the total
cost.
(cid:73) The recurrence tree can be used to generate a value for the
substitution method.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 3
DS 2018/2019
31 / 40

(cid:73) The dimension of a sub-problem corresponding to a node on level i:
n/4i ⇒ sub-problem size becomes n = 1 when
n/4i = 1 ⇔ i = log4n ⇒ the tree has log4n + 1 levels.
(cid:73) The number of nodes on level i: 3i .
(cid:73) The cost of any node on level i : c(n/4i )2.
(cid:73) The total cost of nodes on level i: 3i c(n/4i )2 = (3/16)i cn2
(last level log4n: nlog43T (1).)

4. Master theorem
(cid:73) Provides a method to solve recurrences of type
T (n) = aT (n/b) + f (n) where a ≥ 1 and b > 1 are constants, and
f (n) is a function asymptotically positive.
(cid:73) Master theorem:
Let a ≥ 1 and b > 1 constants, f (n) a function and T (n) deﬁned on
non-negative integer numbers by the recurrence relation:
T (n) = aT (n/b) + f (n). Then:
1. If f (n) = O(nlogba−(cid:15)) for (cid:15) > 0 constant, then T (n) = Θ(nlogba).
2. If f (n) = Θ(nlogba), then T (n) = Θ(nlogba log n).
3. If f (n) = Ω(nlogba+(cid:15)) for (cid:15) > 0 constant, and if af (n/b) ≤ cf (n) for
c < 1 and n large enough, then T (n) = Θ(f (n)).
FII, UAIC
Lecture 3
DS 2018/2019
38 / 40

Master theorem – examples
(cid:73) T (n) = 9T (n/3) + n
a = 9, b = 3, f (n) = n and nlogba = nlog39 = Θ(n2).
Since f (n) = O(nlog39−(cid:15)), with (cid:15) = 1, the case 1 of Master theorem
can be applied ⇒ T (n) = Θ(n2).
(cid:73) T (n) = T (2n/3) + 1
a = 1, b = 3/2, f (n) = 1 and nlogba = nlog3/21 = n0 = 1.
Since f (n) = Θ(nlogba) = Θ(1), the case 2 of Master theorem can be
applied ⇒ T (n) = Θ(log n).
FII, UAIC
Lecture 3
DS 2018/2019
39 / 40

Master theorem – examples
(cid:73) T (n) = 3T (n/4) + n log n
a = 3, b = 4, f (n) = n log n and nlogba = nlog43 = O(n0.793)
Since f (n) = Ω(nlog43+(cid:15)), with (cid:15) ≈ 0.2, the case 3 of Master theorem
can be applied if: af (n/b) = 3(n/4) log(n/4) ≤ (3/4)n log n = cf (n)
for c = 3/4 and n large enough. It follows T (n) = Θ(n log n).
(cid:73) The Master theorem cannot be applied for T (n) = 2T (n/2) + n log n
a = 2, b = 2, f (n) = n log n and nlogba = n
Since f (n) = n log n is asymptotically larger the nlogba = n, it follows
hat the case 3 can be applied (false!!).
f (n) is not polynomial larger.
f (n)/nlogba = (n log n)/n = log n is asymptotically smaller than n(cid:15),
for any positive constant (cid:15).
FII, UAIC
Lecture 3
DS 2018/2019
40 / 40
